_**Agent (default)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The fix is to update `deployBuild.mjs` so it automatically installs or uses the correct Hugo version when the pinned version is unavailable.

Cloudflare Pages defaults to Hugo 0.147.7 when HUGO_VERSION is unset. A dashboard fix would set HUGO_VERSION to 0.163.2. Alternatively, an `ensureHugo()` function could check and install the required version.

The helper would download Hugo extended into `.cache/hugo-$version/` and prepend that path to `PATH`. A dedicated `ensureHugo.mjs` script imported by `deployBuild.mjs` is cleaner than embedding the logic directly. Cloudflare Pages runs on Linux.
</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Cloudflare Pages is using Hugo 0.147.7 instead of the pinned 0.163.2. This version mismatch causes the template execution failure.

The `hugo.Data` API requires Hugo 0.158+, which explains why 0.147.7 fails. The best fix is to update `deployBuild.mjs` to auto-install the correct Hugo version during deployment. This prevents future mismatches without manual dashboard changes.
</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
macOS universal builds only provide `.pkg` installers, which are difficult to extract programmatically.

Auto-installing Hugo is unnecessary on macOS because users typically install it via mise or Homebrew. The plan is to simplify the script: only auto-install on Linux for CI environments, and fail with a clear message on other platforms if the version does not match.
</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Adding `.cache` to `.gitignore` to exclude Hugo binary cache files.

Writing `ensureHugo.mjs` to resolve the pinned Hugo Extended version. It checks PATH first, then downloads to `.cache/hugo/` on Linux or Windows.

Implementing helper functions in `ensureHugo.mjs`. `readPinnedHugoVersion` reads the pinned version from `deploy-versions.json`. `parseHugoVersion` extracts the version string from Hugo's version output. `pathHugoVersion` checks the system PATH for an existing Hugo binary. `cacheDir` returns the local cache directory path.

`cachedBinary` locates the cached Hugo executable in the platform-specific cache subdirectory. `assetForPlatform` selects the correct extended release archive filename based on the current OS and architecture. Next, `downloadUrl` will generate the download URL.

`downloadUrl` builds the GitHub release URL for the selected Hugo archive. `installHugo` creates the cache directory and logs the installation step. It exits with an error if the platform lacks a prebuilt extended binary.

`installHugo` downloads the Hugo archive with curl, extracts it with tar, and removes the temporary archive. It then verifies the cached binary exists.

`installHugo` now validates the extracted binary by checking its version against the pinned release. It sets executable permissions on Linux and exits if the version does not match. `ensureHugo` is being defined to combine the pinned version with the PATH check.

`ensureHugo` now returns the system `hugo` binary when its version matches the pinned release. Otherwise it checks the local cache and uses the pinned binary if it is valid. It warns when the PATH version differs from the pinned version, then falls back to downloading the pinned release. Next, `deployBuild.mjs` will be updated to use this helper.

`deployBuild.mjs` now imports `ensureHugo` and runs the build with the resolved binary. Curl and tar are confirmed available on Cloudflare Pages. The pinned version will be downloaded whenever the system PATH contains an incompatible Hugo version.
</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

The Cloudflare build failed for two related reasons:

1. **Wrong Hugo version** — Cloudflare used **0.147.7** (its default), but the site pins **0.163.2** (extended).
2. **Template error** — `hugo.Data.icons` in `icon.html` needs Hugo ≥ 0.158. On 0.147.7 you get `can't evaluate field Data in type interface {}`.

The build only **warned** about the version mismatch and kept going with the old Hugo, so every page that renders the search icon failed.

## Fix

I added `scripts/ensureHugo.mjs`, which the deploy build now runs first:

- If PATH has the correct pinned Hugo → use it
- If PATH has the wrong version (like Cloudflare’s 0.147.7) → download **Hugo Extended 0.163.2** into `.cache/hugo/`
- If Hugo isn’t on PATH → install the pinned version

I verified this by simulating Cloudflare’s old Hugo on PATH — the build detects the mismatch, installs 0.163.2, and completes successfully.

## What you should do

Push this change and redeploy. The build should pass even without dashboard env vars.

Optional but still worth setting in **Cloudflare Pages → Settings → Environment variables**:

- `HUGO_VERSION=0.163.2`
- `NODE_VERSION=22`

That skips the download step on each build. Your logs also show `npm run build` — `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile && pnpm run deploy` (from `wrangler.toml`) is fine too; either works once Hugo is correct.

_**Agent (default)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The local graph has a `focus` field set.

Pinning the focus node at the center prevents true force-directed layout. The fix removes that pinning so all nodes participate in the force simulation.

Circular initial positions should be replaced with random or scattered ones so the force simulation can spread nodes properly. The prelayout function currently skips its 200 iterations when reduced motion is enabled, which would prevent a static layout from forming.

Pinned focus nodes create a hub-and-spoke radial layout rather than an organic force-directed shape. The fix should run prelayout even under reduced motion preferences so nodes settle into a static configuration. Focus nodes must not be pinned during simulation so every node contributes equally to the force layout.

All graphs should use the same scattered initial positions, removing the special circular setup for focus nodes. The focus node can remain visually distinct without being pinned during simulation. The neighbor filtering in `viewNodes` is a visibility control, not a layout constraint, so it stays. Unpinned focus nodes must still avoid navigation clicks, since the existing logic already excludes them from routing.

Initial node positions will be randomly distributed across the canvas. A padded bounding box centered on the viewport will contain the starting coordinates. The existing circular arrangement is replaced by a scattered seed pattern derived from each node index.

The `simulate()` function will drop its focus-node pinning block entirely. Link forces will no longer exclude the focus node. Nodes will start with random coordinates across the full canvas.

Link force conditions will be simplified to only exclude dragging nodes. For reduced-motion users, prelayout will run 350 iterations to settle the static layout, then freeze simAlpha at zero with no animation loop. The focus-node-specific alpha value becomes less important once the focus node is no longer pinned.

The simulation alpha can be unified to a single value since the focus node is no longer pinned. Link distances may be shortened for local graphs. The viewport will still center on the focus node after the force layout settles. The existing drag restriction that prevents moving the focus node is preserved.

The focus node will fully participate in the force simulation. It remains non-draggable and non-clickable, preserving the local-graph UX where the current page stays fixed while neighbors orbit it.

Checking whether CSS or filter changes trigger a circular re-layout on reset. The full graph page likely already behaves correctly; the local graph may be the only one that looks off due to the pinned focus node.

Reset and resize both call initPositions without running prelayout, which would leave nodes in a circular arrangement. Filter changes correctly skip re-layout. The fix should add prelayout after initialization in both reset and resize paths.
</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Fixing a potential double settleLayout issue during boot.

The double settleLayout is harmless. On first boot, resize() already initializes positions and settles the layout, so boot repeats it once. After that, frame is set to 1, preventing further redundant calls.

Resize events skip fitToView, but ResizeObserver handles it separately. Filter changes correctly avoid re-layout. simulate() returns true while settling, and false once alpha drops below threshold.
</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

All note graphs now share one force-directed layout in `notes-graph.js`:

**What changed**
- **Removed the pinned center node** on local graphs — the current note moves with link and repulsion forces like every other node
- **Replaced the circular starting layout** with scattered seed positions, then ~240 simulation ticks to settle (400 for reduced-motion users, with no animation after)
- **Unified physics** — same link distance, repulsion, and center gravity for the full graph page and per-note local graphs
- **Reset and resize** re-run the full settle pass so layout stays force-directed after those events

Local graphs still highlight the current note and only show its neighborhood; they just arrange organically instead of in a fixed ring around the center.

_**Agent (default)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The public build may be stale. The graph page structure could also differ.

The graph likely freezes because `settleLayout` is too expensive. With ~200 nodes, each layout pass calculates roughly 4.7 million node-pair interactions across 240 iterations. Every resize triggers another full pass, blocking the main thread and making the graph appear broken.

A resize loop may be forming. `resize()` triggers `settleLayout`, which can alter layout and trigger another resize. `boot()` also calls `resize()` during startup, and the initial frame zero forces an immediate layout pass. Previously, prelayout only ran on first load without reduced motion; now it runs on every resize.
</details></think>

const t0=Date.now();

  for(let i=0;i&lt;nodes.length;i++) for(let j=i+1;j&lt;nodes.length;j++){
    const a=nodes[i],b=nodes[j];
    const dx=a.x-b.x, dy=a.y-b.y;
    const dist=Math.sqrt(dx*dx+dy*dy)||1;
  }
  edges.forEach(e=&gt;{
    const dx=e.target.x-e.source.x, dy=e.target.y-e.source.y;

```bash
cd /home/jorap/Documents/GitHub/jorap.com && node -e "
const fs=require('fs');
const html=fs.readFileSync('public/notes/graph/index.html','utf8');
const m=html.match(/notes-graph-data\">(\{[\s\S]*?\})<\/script>/);
const data=JSON.parse(m[1]);
console.log('nodes', data.nodes.length, 'edges', data.edges.length);

// benchmark simulate cost
const nodes=data.nodes.map(n=>({...n,x:100,y:100,vx:0,vy:0,degree:n.totalDegree||0}));
const nodeById={}; nodes.forEach(n=>nodeById[n.id]=n);
const edges=data.edges.map(e=>({source:nodeById[e.source],target:nodeById[e.target]})).filter(e=>e.source&&e.target);
const n=nodes.length;
console.log('pairs per iter', n*(n-1)/2);
const t0=Date.now();
for(let iter=0;iter<240;iter++){
  for(let i=0;i<nodes.length;i++) for(let j=i+1;j<nodes.length;j++){
    const a=nodes[i],b=nodes[j];
    const dx=a.x-b.x, dy=a.y-b.y;
    const dist=Math.sqrt(dx*dx+dy*dy)||1;
  }
  edges.forEach(e=>{
    const dx=e.target.x-e.source.x, dy=e.target.y-e.source.y;
    Math.sqrt(dx*dx+dy*dy);
  });
}
console.log('240 iter ms', Date.now()-t0);
"
```

```bash
cd /home/jorap/Documents/GitHub/jorap.com && node <<'EOF'
const fs=require('fs');
const html=fs.readFileSync('public/notes/graph/index.html','utf8');
const m=html.match(/notes-graph-data>(\{.*\})<\/script>/);
const data=JSON.parse(m[1]);
const nodes=data.nodes.map(n=>({...n,x:0,y:0,vx:0,vy:0,degree:n.totalDegree||0}));
const nodeById={}; nodes.forEach(n=>{nodeById[n.id]=n;});
const edges=data.edges.map(e=>({source:nodeById[e.source],target:nodeById[e.target]})).filter(e=>e.source&&e.target);
const width=800,height=600,pad=48,w=width-pad*2,h=height-pad*2;
function hashSeed(str){let h=2166136261;for(let i=0;i<str.length;i++){h^=str.charCodeAt(i);h=Math.imul(h,16777619);}return (h>>>0)/4294967296;}
nodes.forEach(n=>{n.x=pad+hashSeed(n.id+':x')*w;n.y=pad+hashSeed(n.id+':y')*h;});
function nodeRadius(n){return Math.min(7,2.5+Math.sqrt(n.degree||0)*0.9);}
const t0=Date.now();
for(let iter=0;iter<240;iter++){
  const alpha=1, cx=width/2, cy=height/2;
  const linkDistance=120, linkStrength=0.16*alpha, repulsion=4200*alpha, centerStrength=0.014*alpha;
  edges.forEach(e=>{
    const dx=e.target.x-e.source.x, dy=e.target.y-e.source.y;
    const dist=Math.sqrt(dx*dx+dy*dy)||1;
    const force=((dist-linkDistance)/dist)*linkStrength;
    e.source.vx+=dx/dist*force; e.source.vy+=dy/dist*force;
    e.target.vx-=dx/dist*force; e.target.vy-=dy/dist*force;
  });
  for(let i=0;i<nodes.length;i++) for(let j=i+1;j<nodes.length;j++){
    const a=nodes[i],b=nodes[j];
    const dx=a.x-b.x, dy=a.y-b.y, dist=Math.sqrt(dx*dx+dy*dy)||1;
    const minDist=nodeRadius(a)+nodeRadius(b)+36;
    let repel=repulsion/(dist*dist);
    if(dist<minDist) repel+=((minDist-dist)/dist)*0.65*alpha;
    a.vx+=(dx/dist)*repel; a.vy+=(dy/dist)*repel;
    b.vx-=(dx/dist)*repel; b.vy-=(dy/dist)*repel;
  }
  nodes.forEach(n=>{
    n.vx+=(cx-n.x)*centerStrength; n.vy+=(cy-n.y)*centerStrength;
    n.vx*=0.84; n.vy*=0.84; n.x+=n.vx; n.y+=n.vy;
  });
}
console.log('settle ms', Date.now()-t0);
const xs=nodes.map(n=>n.x), ys=nodes.map(n=>n.y);
console.log('x range', Math.min(...xs), Math.max(...xs));
console.log('y range', Math.min(...ys), Math.max(...ys));
console.log('nan', nodes.some(n=>!Number.isFinite(n.x)||!Number.isFinite(n.y)));
EOF
```

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Running the graph JavaScript in Node.js with jsdom or Puppeteer to diagnose the issue.

The ResizeObserver callback triggers `settleLayout()` on every container resize. That function blocks the main thread for roughly 19 milliseconds per 89 nodes. This likely causes the graph to hang or fail.

The ResizeObserver firing during canvas append is unlikely to crash the graph entirely. A double boot() call via requestAnimationFrame was ruled out as harmless. Now examining the fitToView zoom calculation.

The fitToView zoom calculation caps at 3.6x even when nodes cluster tightly together. Negative Y coordinates across a 600px viewport should still fit correctly. The global graph page incorrectly uses focus centering on "eternal-principles," which forces fitToView to center on that node and restricts neighbor highlighting.

A dedicated graph-data template without focus should be created for the global graph page. The resize handler must stop triggering full re-settlement on every container resize. Large graphs need their layout settling split into chunks.

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The HTML integrity hash decoded from the HTML entity is LtWfFenoPeeUoWAgpvEAwZ9/DMgRzAMigGTh+m5Mso=.

The actual script file hash is fjlD/9/YE/rXjyV1gG1tOuXdNUFte6YDaYfZZLO0AkY=. This does not match the stale HTML integrity hash. The browser will block the script due to this mismatch.

The integrity hash actually matches the file. The problem likely comes from global focus causing fitToView to zoom oddly on a neighbor subset. Another possibility is simulate returning true forever and hanging the browser. A resize observer might also be resetting the layout continuously. The first fix is to stop passing focus on the graph page.

Resize events will no longer trigger re-settlement. Only boot and explicit reset will call fitToView and draw. The graph template will clear the focus field by merging an empty string, which JavaScript treats as null.

The `edges.forEach` loop may double-count node degrees after recent changes, inflating node radius without breaking the graph. The user's report likely targets the force-directed changes specifically.

Removing focus node pinning risks drifting the focused view away from center. settleLayout iterations will scale with node count and cap at a maximum. The resize observer will no longer trigger prelayout; that step only runs on boot or explicit reset. Boot will collapse into a single call to avoid a double-boot race.

The original resize logic only called initPositions and settleLayout when the frame was missing or the size changed. The new code incorrectly triggers settleLayout on every resize. The fix adds a relayout flag to the resize function so it only settles the layout during boot or explicit reset. The ResizeObserver will pass that flag to prevent constant re-settlement.
</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

The force-directed change re-runs full layout on every resize, which breaks the graph. I'll fix that and clear the global `focus` on the graph page so the full map displays.

<summary>Tool use: **run_terminal_command_v2** • Run command: curl -s http://localhost:1313/notes/graph/ | python3 -c &#34;
import sys,re,json
html=sys.stdin.read()
m=re.search(r&#39;class=\&#34;notes-graph-data\&#34;&gt;(.*?)&lt;/script&gt;&#39;, html, re.S)
if m:
    d=json.loads(m.group(1))
    print(&#39;nodes&#39;, len(d.get(&#39;nodes&#39;,[])), &#39;edges&#39;, len(d.get(&#39;edges&#39;,[])))
    print(&#39;focus&#39;, d.get(&#39;focus&#39;))
    print(&#39;mode&#39;, d.get(&#39;mode&#39;))
else:
    print(&#39;no graph data&#39;)
&#34;</summary>
```bash
curl -s http://localhost:1313/notes/graph/ | python3 -c "
import sys,re,json
html=sys.stdin.read()
m=re.search(r'class=\"notes-graph-data\">(.*?)</script>', html, re.S)
if m:
    d=json.loads(m.group(1))
    print('nodes', len(d.get('nodes',[])), 'edges', len(d.get('edges',[])))
    print('focus', d.get('focus'))
    print('mode', d.get('mode'))
else:
    print('no graph data')
"
```

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The local graph uses `mode: "local"` while the global graph uses `mode: "global"`.

JavaScript will only apply focus when mode is local. Resize handling will be refactored to skip unnecessary relayouts. The expand button will sync canvas size and fit to view instead of triggering a full relayout.

`syncCanvasSize` now returns whether the dimensions actually changed. A new `relayout` function handles position initialization and settling. The `resize` function accepts a `relayout` option to control when layout recalculation occurs.

The resize logic is simplified further. A new `relayoutGraph` function handles position initialization and settling. The boot sequence runs `syncCanvasSize`, `relayoutGraph`, and `fitToView`. The ResizeObserver now only resizes the canvas and fits to view. The expand button resizes the canvas and fits to view. The reset handler performs a full relayout. Existing `resize()` calls are being replaced with these targeted functions.

Boot now runs once with an optional deferred `requestAnimationFrame` to refit after fonts and styles settle. The deferred pass skips relayout and only refits if the canvas size changed. On the global graph, the hovered node defaults to null, preventing unwanted dimming.
</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

Fixed two bugs that were breaking the graph at `/notes/graph/`:

**1. Global page was using a “focus” node**  
The graph data always included `focus: "/notes/eternal-principles/"`, even on the full graph page. The JS treated that like a local graph: it zoomed to only that node’s neighbors and dimmed everything else. Most of the map looked blank or broken.

**Fix:** Focus is only used when `mode === "local"` (note-page graphs). On the global graph page, all ~89 nodes show at full opacity and `fitToView` frames the whole graph.

**2. Layout reset on every resize**  
The force-directed change re-ran `initPositions()` + `settleLayout()` whenever the container resized. The `ResizeObserver` could fire repeatedly (especially when the canvas size changed), causing a relayout loop that kept the graph from settling.

**Fix:** Split canvas sizing from layout:
- **Boot / Reset** → full relayout
- **Resize / expand** → resize canvas and refit the view only, keep node positions

Refresh `http://localhost:1313/notes/graph/` — you should see the full interactive graph. Local graphs on individual note pages still center on the current note.
